title: What is JavaScript? 🌐
description: Discover the language that makes websites come alive.

slides.title: Meet JavaScript! 👋
slides.content:
# Welcome to JavaScript!

JavaScript is the **most popular** programming language in the world. It was created by **Brendan Eich** in just **10 days** back in 1995 — and it now powers virtually every website you visit! 🎉

JavaScript is used by companies like **Google, Facebook, Netflix, and Amazon**. It's the go-to language for:

* 🌐 Web Development (front-end & back-end)
* 📱 Mobile Apps (React Native, Ionic)
* 🎮 Game Development
* 🤖 Server-Side Programming (Node.js)
* 🖥️ Desktop Apps (Electron)

slides.title: Why JavaScript is Perfect for Beginners 🌟
slides.content:
## The Language of the Web

Think of a website like a house:

* **HTML** is the **structure** — walls, doors, roof. 🏠
* **CSS** is the **style** — paint, decorations, furniture. 🎨
* **JavaScript** is the **electricity** — lights, appliances, everything that *moves*. ⚡

Without JavaScript, websites would be static pages — like a poster on a wall. JavaScript brings them to **life**!

Here's why developers love it:

1. **Runs Everywhere**: Every web browser has a built-in JavaScript engine.
2. **No Setup Needed**: Open your browser → open the console → start coding!
3. **Instant Results**: See your changes immediately in the browser.
4. **Huge Community**: Millions of developers and thousands of free packages.
5. **One Language, Many Uses**: Web, mobile, desktop, servers — JavaScript does it all.

quizData.question: Who created JavaScript?
quizData.explanation: Brendan Eich created JavaScript in 1995 in just 10 days! Guido van Rossum created Python.

slides.title: JavaScript vs. Java ☕❌
slides.content:
## They're NOT the Same!

This is the **#1 beginner confusion**: JavaScript and Java are **completely different** languages! 🤯

Think of it like **car** and **carpet** — they share letters, but they're totally unrelated.

| Feature | JavaScript | Java |
|---------|-----------|------|
| Created by | Brendan Eich | James Gosling |
| Year | 1995 | 1995 |
| Runs in | Browsers + Node.js | JVM (Java Virtual Machine) |
| Typing | Dynamic | Static |
| Use case | Web pages | Enterprise software |

The name similarity was actually a **marketing strategy** — Java was super popular in 1995, so Netscape renamed their language from "Mocha" to "JavaScript" to ride the hype! 📈

quizData.question: What role does JavaScript play in a website?

options.text: It stores data on the server

quizData.explanation: JavaScript adds interactivity to websites — it's the electricity that makes everything move and respond! HTML provides structure, CSS provides style.

slides.title: How JavaScript Works ⚙️
slides.content:
## Under the Hood

Every web browser comes with a **JavaScript engine** built right in:

* **Chrome** → V8 Engine 🏎️
* **Firefox** → SpiderMonkey 🕷️
* **Safari** → JavaScriptCore 🍎
* **Edge** → V8 Engine (same as Chrome!)

When you open a web page, the engine:

1. **Reads** the JavaScript code.
2. **Parses** it into instructions the computer understands.
3. **Executes** it instantly — no compilation step needed! ⚡

This means JavaScript is an **interpreted** language (with modern Just-In-Time compilation for speed).

slides.title: Beyond the Browser 🤔
slides.content:
## Node.js Changed Everything

In 2009, **Ryan Dahl** took Chrome's V8 engine and created **Node.js** — allowing JavaScript to run **outside** the browser on servers! 🖥️

This was revolutionary. Before Node.js, you needed to learn different languages for:

* **Front-end** (browser) → JavaScript
* **Back-end** (server) → PHP, Python, Ruby

Now with Node.js, you can use **JavaScript for everything**! This made it the first truly **full-stack** language.

quizData.question: What is the name of Chrome's JavaScript engine?
quizData.explanation: Chrome uses the V8 JavaScript engine, which is also used by Node.js and Microsoft Edge.

slides.title: Let's Get Started! 🛠️
slides.content:
## You're Ready!

You now know what JavaScript is, why it dominates the web, and how it works behind the scenes.

In the next lesson, we'll write your **very first JavaScript program** — and you'll see results on screen instantly.

Get ready to say `console.log('Hello, World!')` — JavaScript style! 🚀
